narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in academic pathways like this. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 3, a Year 3 decile of 3, middle family income, an LSAT score of 32, an undergraduate GPA of 2.50, and plans to attend full-time, which is the typical path for most students. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' bar exam outcomes, predicted a 43.6% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try—above the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as schools prioritize those likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pulling against failure was your LSAT score, which the model saw as a stronger signal of potential than average, nudging the prediction toward passing. However, your Year 3 decile rank stood out as the top driver toward a failure prediction, as it was notably lower than the typical student's mid-range performance, raising concerns about sustained academic momentum. Other elements like your family income and Year 1 decile offered some positive lean toward passing by aligning with resilient backgrounds the model associates with success, while your GPA had a minor push toward failure compared to higher averages. Your full-time status helped slightly, matching the vast majority who thrive in that structure. Compared to typical applicants—who boast higher average LSATs around 37, GPAs near 3.2, and deciles in the 5-6 range—your metrics placed you below the middle of the pack in key academic areas, tipping the scales. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential, but a data-driven call based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that standout strength, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or programs supporting underrepresented students like you, and reapplying with an updated profile that showcases growth. Many have turned similar predictions around—your story is far from over. Keep pushing; law needs voices like yours.
